Recent studies have proposed to prune transformers in an unexplainable manner.

The proposed novel explainable pruning framework dubbed X-Pruner improves the explainability of the pruning criterion.
An explainability-aware mask is proposed to measure each prunable unit's contribution to predicting each target class.
The explainability-aware mask values differentiate between unpruned and pruned units for preserving the most informative units.
Adaptive search is performed to find the layer-wise threshold that differentiates between unpruned and pruned units based on explainability-aware mask values.
The X-Pruner method is applied on representative transformer models including the DeiT and Swin Transformer.
The proposed X-Pruner outperforms the state-of-the-art black-box methods with reduced computational costs and slight performance degradation.

Pruning for transformers has been categorized into unstructured and structured pruning methods.
Unstructured pruning techniques include magnitude-based and hessian-based approaches.
Unstructured pruning results in irregular sparsity, causing sparse tensor computations that are difficult to align.
Sparse tensor computations are affected by irregular sparsity, making it difficult to align with hardware efficiency.
Structured pruning involves removing uninformative contiguous structures of a pre-trained model to alleviate certain problems.
Attention heads and rows of weight matrix are examples of uninformative contiguous structures that can be pruned for model optimization.
Structured dropout selects specific sub-structures of a model during the inference time for optimization purposes.
Model compression techniques were applied to a range of pre-trained language models, resulting in impressive results as discussed by Liu et al.

Yu et al. proposed a structured pruning method for vision transformers involving a 0/1 mask to differentiate important and unimportant parameters.
The structured pruning method uses a differentiable threshold to differentiate parameters, but the resulting mask is non-differentiable, potentially impacting gradient bias and weight optimization.
Gradients being biased could potentially result in suboptimal results of the remaining weights.
